content.assessment: This proposal aligns most closely with the governance posture already signaled by the Education Minister, who explicitly favors admissions-mechanism dialogue over structural abolition, suggesting a lower legal-authority threshold since it operates within the existing statutory track structure. However, enforceability is constrained by the same maintainer-autonomy barrier: church-maintained and private schools outside Klebelsberg Centre authority may resist or opt out, limiting the reform's national coverage and creating a two-tier accountability regime.
opportunities: Framing reform as procedural rather than structural may reduce the mobilization capacity of opposition actors who previously reframed lesser proposals as existential threats.
strengths: Directly matches the reform pathway articulated by the sitting Education Minister, reducing legal-political friction relative to structural abolition.
Precedent exists for admissions-level intervention producing measurable segregation reduction without requiring new track-structure legislation.
National Pedagogue Chamber's proposed institution-by-institution accountability review offers a concrete, already-advocated enforceability mechanism.
threats: Historical stagnation of 2000s desegregation measures after initial gains suggests reform fatigue or institutional reversal risk absent sustained enforcement authority.
Fragmented maintainer compliance could produce an enforcement patchwork undermining the rights-compatibility goal of equal treatment across the national system.
weaknesses: Church-maintained and minority self-government schools' independence from Klebelsberg Centre authority means admissions reform cannot achieve full national coverage through ministerial action alone.
Divided union positions (PSZ vs PDSZ) signal absent professional consensus, weakening political durability of implementation.
